headline: Sidney Edgerton's Term as First Territorial Governor Ends, 1866
body:
Well now, if you ever heard tell of a man wrestling a greased pig in a mud pit, you'd have a fair notion of the task that fell to Sidney Edgerton. On this very day, the eleventh of July in the year of our Lord eighteen hundred and sixty-six, his term as the first Territorial Governor of Montana finally sputtered to a close. Appointed by none other than Honest Abe Lincoln himself back in '64, Edgerton was sent out to a land wilder than a cat in a bathtub, a place where gold fever ran hotter than a blacksmith's forge and law was mostly a suggestion whispered on the wind. 

Before Montana was even a twinkle in the federal eye, it was just the eastern half of Idaho Territory, a place so sprawling and disjointed it made about as much sense as a square wheel on a wagon. A thousand miles separated Lewiston, the capital, from the mining camps out east, and those miners, bless their gold-addled hearts, knew a thing or two about being ignored. They figured Lewiston, hundreds of miles away over mountains choked with snow, couldn't govern a flea, let alone a territory bursting at the seams with fortune-seekers and desperadoes. So they started kicking up a fuss, demanding their own patch of dirt, their own government. 

Then along came Judge Sidney Edgerton in September of '63, a former Ohio congressman, meant to be chief justice of Idaho. But winter caught him in Bannack, and he soon learned the Idaho governor had given him the cold shoulder, assigning him to a far-off judicial district. Edgerton, along with his nephew Wilbur Fisk Sanders, took up the miners' cry. He knew President Lincoln, you see, and a good many congressmen besides. So, with two thousand dollars in gold jingling in his pockets, a gift from the hopeful miners, he headed east in January '64 to plead their case in Washington. 

Lincoln, being Lincoln, listened, and on June 27, 1864, Edgerton got the nod: first governor of Montana Territory. His first order of business? Naming a capital. And where did he pick? Bannack, naturally. It was where he lived, and a man's got to have his comforts, even when he's trying to tame a wilderness. 

But oh, the troubles that followed! The territory went sixteen months without a territorial secretary who could sign federal warrants. That meant Governor Edgerton couldn't spend a dime of federal money. So, like any good frontiersman, he dug into his own pockets, hoping against hope he'd be reimbursed someday. He paid for the very foundations of government out of his own hide, a testament to his stubbornness, if nothing else. 

Lawlessness was rampant, of course. Gold camps like Bannack and Virginia City were magnets for every sort of character, good, bad, and downright ugly. The Vigilantes, a rough-and-ready bunch, took matters into their own hands, doling out justice with a rope and a tree branch. Edgerton, some say, was even among their number, a silent partner in the grim business of bringing order to chaos. 

Then, in late September '65, a secretary finally arrived: Thomas Francis Meagher, appointed by Lincoln's successor, President Andrew Johnson. Edgerton, perhaps weary of the whole affair, turned the reins over to Meagher, making him acting governor. He then lit out for Ohio, with a stop in Washington D.C. to attend to personal business and Montana concerns. President Johnson, a man who had no love for Republicans like Edgerton, saw his chance and took it. 

So, on this day, the curtain fell on Sidney Edgerton's time as governor. He'd laid the groundwork, established the first capital, even if it did move from Bannack to Virginia City and then, eventually, to Helena. He'd wrestled with the wildness of the land and the wilder nature of men, all while keeping a hand in his own pocket. Montana, in its very shape and name, owes a debt to that Ohio gentleman, a debt paid in gold dust, political wrangling, and the sheer grit of a man trying to make sense of a new world. The echoes of those early days, of the gold camps and the struggle for law, still whisper through the canyons, a story as rich and untamed as the land itself.
